So this is the workflow, and we've basically broken down the process into chunks.
So we start by providing it with the topic.
So the topic can be either the target keyword or the title of the article.
And at this stage, the input is the...
So we give it an assignment direction.
The reason is that we give it an assignment direction.
I don't know if you've ever tried to generate an article in ChatGPT or Claude, and you just told it, write me an article about this topic.
And you get an article that's pretty short, and it kind of like is all over the place.

And that's because they don't like, LLMs don't really know what a good article structure is like, they're not going to go to the first page of Google and be like, okay, what should this article actually cover to meet the search intent and rank in Google search and to be comprehensive for other LLMs.

So what we do here is that we will manually like research this topic in Google and come back with a list of topics to cover that it has to cover in the article, but it also, we say, okay, don't limit yourself only to these topics.
And we also explain what the conclusion should include.
And we also specify that it should include an FAQ.

Q section for LLM optimization, and we also provide special instructions in the sense of, like, if there's anything, you know, because the topic of browser sandboxing is quite broad, so we want to say, okay, what is there, is there a special angle that we want to insert here that's unique to Blaxel, that other companies, that other websites might not be talking about.
And then, once we're happy with the assignment direction, we run the researcher, and our researcher, we use EXA.
We used to use perplexity in the past, but perplexity just wasn't advanced enough, and, like, our researcher can find really high-quality information.

Sometimes it might even find information in gated reports, which can sometimes be tricky, because it's, like, we get a stat, and we know that the stat is true, but it's actually behind a PDF that we can...
So we either need to find a way to download a PDF or we just have to remove it because we never want to provide stats or third-party research in a blog that we can't verify is true.
So UXA is like a really good researcher and it's what enables us to write really technical content and make sure that it's accurate.
So you are using UXA to download the PDFs, not only for that, but also to download PDFs and doing like OCR and analyzing the content of the PDFs.
Yeah, yeah.

So what the process looks like is we, the researcher takes a look at the assignment direction and then it creates a list of questions that it has to answer in order for our workflow to have enough information to write this topic.
So these are for you.
For the research questions that it came up with for this topic, like, what is browser sandboxing?
How does that work technically?
And then it will summarize the finding, and it's going to provide links to the different sources.
And there are many different questions here, like, what's the difference?
And there are, like, lots of links here.
And what's really helpful about this is that if we're not sure about a specific claim, we can always go back to the researcher and be like, okay, this is where this piece of information came from.
Or if you're ever reviewing a draft and you're like, where did this come from?
Like, why did we insert this?
We can go back to the research and be like, this is what we found.
So it allows us to kind of really go back in and verify.
So essentially, we are not, EXA is kind of summarizing this information for us and providing the links, but they have all of the data, like, on their servers.
And then once the...
researcher is done.
It's going to draft the article.
At this stage, it's going to apply the writing guidelines, but we will typically at this stage, like we go through the content, but we reserve any intense manual edits for the very end.
So we just kind of go through to make sure that the structure is on point and, you know, that it's following like the assignment direction.
And from there, we add the internal links.
This is helpful if we want to, like, programmatically link to other content on your website, but we also add the internal links manually.
And then we also add source links to any claims that we pull, directly pulled from the researcher.
So if we referenced any statistics or third-party reports, it's going, this step is going to go in and provide.
links to those reports so that we can verify those claims.
Okay.

And it also says, like, all links added are to official product documentation, peer-reviewed research, or authoritative security sources as required by the rules, no competitor links or commercial content added.
This is very important.
We don't want to link to your competitors or just link to promotional content.
After this stage, we run the fact checker.
The fact checker basically will break up the article into chunks, and then it's going to ask questions about each chunk and verify it against the researcher.
So if there are any, for example, let me show you actually in studio.
Let me show show show show
Okay, so this is the draft article, and it's going to split it into multiple chunks, and then it's going to extract passages.

So, for example, browser sandboxing does this and this and this, and that it provides a reason for why it extracted that passage for fact-checking, and then it asks questions that it has to answer in order to fact-check this claim.

So, it does that for each piece of information it identifies as needing fact-checking, and when the fact-checker is done researching any piece of information that is incorrect, it's going to go in and basically rewrite the information.
If it cannot verify that something is true, it might rewrite the sentence in a way that signals to us that the fact checker couldn't confirm 100% if something was true.
It might rewrite it in a way that says, while industry best practices support this claim, there is no third-party research to confirm that this best practice does whatever.
So that's just like a dog whistle to us to know that the fact checker couldn't verify something 100%.
This step, validate Blaxel mentions.
This compares all Blaxel mentions against the products and services matrix to make sure that we're not making stuff up about Blaxel.
What, the thing with AI is that it doesn't, it never like intentionally hallucinates, but sometimes the tone, like the,
Writing guidelines might influence how we talk about a specific product.
So this is what we did with Hex, for example.
With Hex, they really want to do, they're a data company, and they want to have a more relaxed tone, like that's more conversational.
But when we tried to do this, the AI would not dumb down, but it would rewrite sections so that they weren't providing as much information as it was before.

So what we have to do at this stage is ensure that we are, you know, when the product is being mentioned, it's being mentioned in a way that the AI isn't assuming that you can do things because other companies can do it.
Like, it has to make sure Blaxel can do this.
This isn't a product and services matrix.
Sometimes this happens because, like, the AI might assume because a company is, like, a compliance platform.
Oh, they have this feature because other...
Companies in this space also have this feature, but that might not be true.
So that's kind of one of the things that we have to watch out for.

And this is a feature that, like an artifact that we started using like a couple of months ago, because it was clear that AI could sometimes, you know, just kind of assume things that companies could do that they really couldn't.
